title: Graph Density Inequalities, Sums of Squares and Tropicalization
speaker: Annie Raymond
abstract: Establishing inequalities among graph densities is a central pursuit in extremal graph theory. One way to certify the nonnegativity of a graph density expression is to write it as a sum of squares or as a rational sum of squares. In this talk, we will explore how one does so and we will then identify simple conditions under which a graph density expression cannot be a sum of squares or a rational sum of squares. Tropicalization will play a key role for the latter. This is joint work with Greg Blekherman, Mohit Singh, and Rekha Thomas.

proof_stages.label: Graph-density profiles as inequality domains
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up homomorphism densities, compares them with subgraph and induced densities, defines graph profiles as closures of density vectors, and frames extremal graph theory as proving polynomial inequalities on these profiles.

proof_stages.label: SOS certificates and graph-SOS motivation
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing real-polynomial SOS, Hilbert's non-SOS phenomenon, and Artin's rational-SOS theorem, the speaker contrasts the graph-density setting where exact and rational graph SOS can fail even though approximate SOS certificates exist.

proof_stages.label: Partial labels, symmetrization, and gluing calculus
proof_stages.summary: Graph sums of squares are made operational by introducing partially labeled homomorphism densities, unlabeling as symmetrization, multiplication as gluing along equal labels, and then using these rules to build a sample certificate.

proof_stages.label: Lowest-degree obstruction and degree-three classification
proof_stages.summary: The key SOS weakness is that the lowest-degree homogeneous part cannot be fixed by cancellations from other degrees, which enables a classification of degree-three graph SOS and proves that the path-of-length-three inequality is nonnegative but not graph SOS, with consequences for Sidorenko-type inequalities and rational variants.

proof_stages.label: Tropicalized graph profiles and binomial inequalities
proof_stages.summary: The graph profile is logarithmically tropicalized into a closed convex cone that preserves valid binomial inequalities, giving tractable descriptions in families such as clique densities and paths while discarding non-binomial boundary information.

proof_stages.label: SOS-profile tropicalization as a rational-SOS obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines degree-bounded SOS profiles via moment matrices of partially labeled graphs, identifies the tropicalization of the SOS profile with a two-by-two-minor relaxation, and uses this relaxed cone to rule out rational graph-SOS representations.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion emphasizes homogeneity as a structural weakness of SOS methods, formulates open questions about rational polyhedrality and decidability for tropical graph profiles, and suggests alternative certificates such as sums of nonnegative circuits.
